title: Content Security Policy Bypass Patterns and Resilient CSP Design
excerpt: Specialist-focused guidance on common CSP bypass classes and policy engineering that survives modern frontend complexity.
content:
# Content Security Policy Bypass Patterns and Resilient CSP Design

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: frontend leads, AppSec engineers, platform defenders
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
CSP can reduce XSS impact only when policy structure is deliberate and continuously tested. Many deployments fail because legacy allowances and framework exceptions quietly re-open script execution channels. Attackers do not break CSP directly; they exploit weak directives, unsafe fallbacks, and trusted domains with dynamic content.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: authenticated browser sessions, admin interfaces, privileged UI flows
- Entry points: reflected and stored script injection points, third-party script trust chains
- Trust boundaries: server policy headers, browser enforcement model, frontend build output
- Typical attacker goals: arbitrary script execution and session abuse

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers test nonce reuse, unsafe-inline fallbacks, overly broad script-src hosts, and JSONP-capable trusted origins. They leverage upload endpoints and CDN subpaths to host attacker-controlled payloads under whitelisted domains. They combine DOM-based sinks with policy gaps such as permissive object-src or missing base-uri constraints.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: directive-by-directive policy review and sink inventory
- Dynamic checks: CSP violation reporting validation and controlled payload suites
- Telemetry indicators: repeated blocked script attempts from unusual origins
- False positive control: confirm actual execution path, not only policy warning

## Secure Implementation
Adopt strict-dynamic with rotating nonces where feasible, remove unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval in production builds, and isolate untrusted content into separate origins. Keep policy generation in CI with regression tests and enforce report-to pipelines for drift detection.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Nonce or hash based script controls
- [x] No unsafe-inline in production policy
- [x] Tight script-src and connect-src origin scopes
- [x] report-to and monitoring for policy violations
- [x] CSP test cases integrated in release pipeline

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: XSS defense and browser hardening guidance
- CWE: CWE-79, CWE-1021
- WSTG: client-side and content security testing
- ASVS: browser-side control requirements

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Content_Security_Policy_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://portswigger.net/web-security/cross-site-scripting/content-security-policy
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
author: Security Team

title: Password Reset Poisoning: Host Header Abuse and Link Integrity
excerpt: Detailed treatment of reset-flow poisoning through host confusion and insecure URL construction.
content:
# Password Reset Poisoning: Host Header Abuse and Link Integrity

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: authentication service owners and backend developers
- Difficulty: practitioner
- Estimated read time: 13 minutes

## Why This Matters
Password reset is one of the most sensitive unauthenticated flows in any web application. If reset links are generated using attacker-controlled host values, account takeover becomes straightforward. Security teams frequently harden login while leaving recovery workflows weak.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: user accounts, administrative access, identity trust
- Entry points: forgot-password endpoint and email template generation logic
- Trust boundaries: inbound HTTP headers to outbound reset email links
- Typical attacker goals: capture reset tokens and take over victim accounts

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers manipulate host and forwarding headers to influence absolute URL generation. The victim receives a valid-looking reset email that points to attacker infrastructure, where token values are harvested. Attackers then replay tokens against legitimate endpoints before expiry.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: trace all reset URL construction logic and proxy header trust rules
- Dynamic checks: header poisoning tests across edge, proxy, and app layers
- Telemetry indicators: reset requests with unusual host metadata patterns
- False positive control: verify real token capture and replay feasibility

## Secure Implementation
Use canonical application origin values from trusted configuration, never from request headers. Sign and bind reset tokens to intended audience and short lifetime. Invalidate all outstanding reset tokens after successful password change and emit high-signal alerts for suspicious reset activity.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Static trusted origin for reset links
- [x] Strict proxy header validation rules
- [x] Short-lived single-use reset tokens
- [x] Post-reset token invalidation and session revocation
- [x] Detection rules for anomalous reset campaigns

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: authentication and secure recovery guidance
- CWE: CWE-640, CWE-601
- WSTG: account recovery testing techniques
- ASVS: identity lifecycle security controls

## References
1. https://portswigger.net/web-security/host-header
2. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Forgot_Password_Cheat_Sheet.html
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/640.html
author: Security Team

title: SAML Security Pitfalls: Signature Wrapping and Trust Configuration
excerpt: Specialist-level SAML hardening guide covering signature validation, canonicalization, and assertion trust boundaries.
content:
# SAML Security Pitfalls: Signature Wrapping and Trust Configuration

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: identity platform engineers and enterprise security teams
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 16 minutes

## Why This Matters
SAML delivers enterprise interoperability, but many implementations assume library defaults are safe. Signature validation mistakes and weak trust configuration can let attackers inject forged assertions or bypass expected audience checks. Because SAML failures affect centralized identity, blast radius is high.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: enterprise SSO sessions, privileged applications, federation trust
- Entry points: assertion consumer service endpoints and metadata ingestion
- Trust boundaries: identity provider signatures to service provider authorization
- Typical attacker goals: impersonation and unauthorized role acquisition

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers attempt XML signature wrapping, namespace confusion, and reference resolution abuse. They exploit weak certificate validation, stale metadata, and permissive audience settings. They target parser differences between validation and business logic extraction paths.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: full assertion validation order and trusted element selection
- Dynamic checks: crafted wrapped assertions and audience mismatch probes
- Telemetry indicators: assertion anomalies and impossible identity provider patterns
- False positive control: verify end-to-end privilege effect in controlled federation test

## Secure Implementation
Use mature SAML libraries with strict XML parsing settings, pin trust anchors, and validate exactly the signed assertion consumed by business logic. Enforce clock skew limits, audience restrictions, and metadata freshness checks.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Strict XML parser configuration and external entity controls
- [x] Signature and reference validation before claim extraction
- [x] Audience, issuer, and time-bound claim enforcement
- [x] Metadata rotation and trust anchor lifecycle management
- [x] Integration tests for wrapped and malformed assertion cases

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: identity protocol implementation guidance
- CWE: CWE-347, CWE-20
- WSTG: authentication and protocol testing
- ASVS: federated identity verification controls

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/SAML_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/347.html
author: Security Team

title: XXE in Document Pipelines: Parser Hardening and Out-of-Band Detection
excerpt: A practical and specialist-oriented guide to preventing and testing XXE in modern parsing and transformation services.
content:
# XXE in Document Pipelines: Parser Hardening and Out-of-Band Detection

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: backend developers handling XML and document conversion
- Difficulty: practitioner
- Estimated read time: 14 minutes

## Why This Matters
XXE continues to appear in internal conversion services and legacy XML integrations where default parser behavior remains dangerous. Even if direct file disclosure is blocked, out-of-band interactions can expose SSRF and internal network metadata.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: local files, cloud metadata endpoints, internal services
- Entry points: XML upload APIs, SOAP adapters, office-to-XML conversion pipelines
- Trust boundaries: untrusted document content to parser and resolver layers
- Typical attacker goals: data exfiltration, SSRF pivoting, service disruption

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers introduce external entity references and parameter entities to force parser resolution. They test blind channels through DNS and HTTP callbacks when direct response exfiltration is blocked. They chain XXE with server-side fetch capabilities for broader network reconnaissance.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: parser configuration for DTD and external entity handling
- Dynamic checks: inline and blind XXE payload sets with OOB listeners
- Telemetry indicators: unexpected resolver requests and metadata endpoint touches
- False positive control: prove parser-level entity resolution from controlled payload

## Secure Implementation
Disable DTD processing and external entity resolution across all parser code paths, including libraries used by conversion toolchains. Validate content type and schema before parsing, and isolate conversion workers from sensitive network zones.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] DTD and external entity resolution disabled
- [x] Blind XXE OOB tests in security regression suite
- [x] XML parser wrappers with safe defaults
- [x] Worker isolation from metadata and internal admin networks
- [x] Alerting on unusual outbound resolver traffic

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: XML security and server-side request risk guidance
- CWE: CWE-611, CWE-918
- WSTG: XXE and SSRF testing methodology
- ASVS: secure parser and input handling controls

## References
1. https://portswigger.net/web-security/xxe
2. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/XML_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/611.html
author: Security Team

title: API Rate Limiting Bypass: Distributed Abuse, Token Spraying, and Control Design
excerpt: Advanced defensive architecture for designing resilient rate limits against modern bot and distributed abuse campaigns.
content:
# API Rate Limiting Bypass: Distributed Abuse, Token Spraying, and Control Design

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: API platform teams, reliability engineers, fraud defense teams
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
Rate limiting failures rarely come from missing limits; they come from weak keying strategies and poor coordination between edge and application layers. Attackers distribute requests across IPs, credentials, devices, and routes to stay below individual thresholds while exhausting backend resources.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: authentication endpoints, search APIs, checkout and inventory services
- Entry points: high-cost read APIs and state-changing operations
- Trust boundaries: CDN or gateway limits to application-level abuse detection
- Typical attacker goals: brute force, scraping, denial of wallet, inventory abuse

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers spray traffic over rotating infrastructure, vary headers and session attributes, and exploit route-specific limit gaps. They abuse asynchronous processing and weak queue backpressure to trigger expensive workloads while appearing compliant per-node.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: review all limit keys, windows, and scope across endpoints
- Dynamic checks: distributed client simulation and multi-identity abuse patterns
- Telemetry indicators: high aggregate cost with low per-identity thresholds
- False positive control: correlate token, device, and behavioral signals before block

## Secure Implementation
Use layered controls that combine identity, token, route, and behavioral dimensions. Apply adaptive limits to high-risk actions and enforce consistent decisions through shared state in low-latency data stores. Couple limits with queue protection and circuit breakers for expensive operations.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Multi-dimensional rate limit keys
- [x] Consistent global enforcement across nodes
- [x] Adaptive throttling for suspicious behavior
- [x] Backpressure and cost-aware request governance
- [x] Abuse simulation tests in pre-production

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: API abuse and brute force defense guidance
- CWE: CWE-307, CWE-770
- WSTG: authorization and abuse-case testing
- ASVS: availability and abuse resistance controls

## References
1. https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0x11-t10/
2. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/REST_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/307.html
author: Security Team
